Facts.text: Thutmose III ascends to the throne of Egypt, although power effectively shifts to Hatshepsut (according to the Low Chronology of the 18th Dynasty)
The Greeks enter Troy using the Trojan Horse (traditional date)
Bishop Ambrose of Milan baptizes Augustine of Hippo
Nicholas I succeeds Benedict III as pope
Halley's Comet sparks English monk to predict country will be destroyed
Battle at Danoura: Yoshitsune Minamoto's fleet beats the Taira during Japan's Genpei War
Jews of Troyes France are accused of ritual murder
General Malik Kafur returns to Delhi after campaign in South India
Pope Urban V names John van Virneburg as Bishop of Utrecht
Fra Mauro completes his Map of the World in Venice for King Alfonso V of Portugal - largest known world map from Medieval Europe and the first to show Africa as a free-standing continent
Battles between Spanish troops & followers of Sultan Suleiman
Pacificatie of Ireland drawn

Piedmontese Easter Massacre: Troops of Charles Emmanuel II, Duke of Savoy, slaughter upwards of 4,000 Waldensian civilians for non-compliance with the Edict of 25 January 1655, which called for the expulsion or conversion of non-Catholic householders in Piedmont, Duchy of Savoy
First continuously published newspaper in America, The Boston News-Letter, is published in Boston by John Campbell
US Library of Congress is established with a $5,000 allocation
Eugene Scribes opera "Le Menteur Veridique" premieres in Paris
Jacob Evert and George Dulty patent the first soda fountain
Inventor and engineer Squire Temple of Utica, New York, granted a patent for an iron truss bridge
Skirmish at Okolona and Birmingham, Mississippi (Grierson's Raid)
Fire alarm & police telegraph system put into operation in San Francisco
Black demonstrators stage ride-ins on Richmond, Virginia streetcars
Mount Vesuvius erupts in Italy
Last federal occupying troops withdraw from the South (New Orleans)
Russo-Turkish War: Russia declares war on the Ottoman Empire
Amateur Athletic Association, the governing body for men's athletics in England and Wales, is founded in Oxford, England
National Medical Association of Black physicians organizes in Atlanta
Lave Cross becomes 1st Philadelphia Phillies player to hit for the cycle in 22-5 win over the Bridegrooms at Eastern Park, Brooklyn
First reporter is assigned to the White House, William Price from the "Washington Star"
First game in baseball's American League: Chicago White Stockings win against the Cleveland Blues 8-2, other games rained out
Senators execute a triple-play & beat Yankees 4-3

Mr & Mrs Jacob Murdock and their children depart Los Angeles in a Packard Thirty, endeavoring to become 1st family to travel across United States by car; arrive in NYC 32 days, 5 hours and 25 minutes later
Harry Hillman and Lawson Robertson run a 100-yard three-legged race in 11 seconds
German Catholic youth movement Quickborn forms
A shipment of 35,000 rifles and 5 million rounds of ammunition are landed at Larne for the Ulster Volunteer Force (UVF, an Ulster loyalist paramilitary group in Northern Ireland
Leaders of the Armenian community in Constantinople (now Istanbul) are arrested by Ottoman authorities, and many later killed, marking the start of the Armenian Genocide
Pittsburgh Rebels' Frank Allen no-hits St Louis Terriers (Federal League), 2-0
WWI: German army fires chloroform gas at Canadian forces near the village of St. Julien, France
NY Yankees left-handed pitcher George Mogridge no-hits Boston Red Sox, 2-1 at Fenway Park
US Congress passes the Liberty Loan Act, authorizing the Treasury to issue a public subscription for 2 billion in bonds for the war
League of Nations assigns British Mandate over Palestine at the San Remo Conference, in San Remo, Italy
Polish troops attack Ukraine
First municipal elections for men and women in Belgium
Under Allies supervision, a plebiscite in the Tyrol favors merging with Germany; unhappy with the outcome, Allies give the area to Italy
General harbor strike begins in NYC
Thorvald Stauning becomes prime minister of Denmark for the first time
88°F highest temperature ever recorded in Cleveland in April
Fathometer, which measures underwater depth, is patented
First non-stop flight from England to India takes off
German national election (NSDAP 36.3% in Prussia)
British army begins evacuation of Greece
Dutch Prince Bernhard becomes an RAF pilot
2nd night Exeter bombed by German Luftwaffe
1st Boeing B-29 arrives in China "over the Hump"
Albert B "Happy" Chandler is named 2nd baseball commissioner
3rd Tony Awards: "Death of a Salesman" & "Kiss Me Kate" win
Independent Republic of South Maluku is declared
Jordan formally annexes the West Bank
US President Harry Truman denies there are communists in the US government
1st American, civilian pilot, P.R. Holden, wounded in Indochina
Australia & USSR break diplomatic relations
WSEE TV channel 35 in Erie, PA (CBS) begins broadcasting
Conference of Bandung against colonialism and for self determination, ends
Gaullists lose elections in France
KFDM TV channel 6 in Beaumont, TX (CBS) begins broadcasting
KMAU (now KGMV) TV channel 3 in Wailuku, HI (CBS) begins broadcasting
AL umpire Frank Umont is 1st to wear glasses in a regular season game
Chicago Cub pitchers walk NL record 9 Reds in 5th inning
Lee Walls hits 3 HRs, as Cubs beat Dodgers 15-2
Netherland Dance Theater opens (Rudi of Dantzig & Cut Flier)
WICD TV channel 15 in Champaign, IL (NBC/ABC) begins broadcasting
14th Tony Awards: "The Miracle Worker" (play) & "Fiorello!" (musical) win
Heavy earthquake strikes South Persia, 500 killed
Record 4 grand slams hit today
First Lockheed A-12 undergoes taxi testing
Massachusetts Institute of Technology sends a TV signal by satellite for the first time from California to Massachusetts
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Mexico becomes a signatory to the Buenos Aires copyright treaty
Military coup under Donald Reid Cabral in Dominican Republic
Atlanta Braves win NL-record 18 straight home games (17 in Milwaukee)
ABC Masters Bowling Tournament won by Pete Tountas
Leftist students take over Columbia University, NYC
Mauritius becomes a member state of the United Nations
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
Car firm British Leyland launch the Austin Maxi in Oporto Portugal
Lebanese army in battle with Palestinians
Loyalist members of the Ulster Volunteer Force (UVF) and the Ulster Protestant Volunteers (UPV) explode a bomb at a water pipeline between Lough Neagh and Belfast, Northern Ireland
Marshall Lin Biao named Mao's designated successor as the sole Vice Chairman of the Communist Party of China
US B-52's drop 3,000 ton bombs at Cambodian boundary
Gambia becomes a republic within the Commonwealth
People's Republic of China launches its 1st satellite transmitting song "East is Red"
Soyuz 10 returns to Earth
Dutch women hockey team becomes world champion
NFL grants franchise to Tampa Bay Buccaneers
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Rhodesian bishop Abel Muzorewa wins general election
US military operation to rescue 52 hostages in Iran fails, 8 die
San Antonio blocks 20 Golden State shots to set an NBA regular season game record
US ends grain embargo against USSR
Cards win 12th game in a row; 7-4 over Phillies
Firestone World Bowling Tournament of Champions won by Mike Durbin
Austrian socialist party loses parliamentary election
Pulitzer prize awarded to Carolyn Lizer for "Yin"
Film "Crocodile Dundee" starring Paul Hogan and Linda Kozlowski premieres in Australia (highest grossing film of the year in the US)
NFL Draft: Auburn tight end Aundray Bruce first pick by Atlanta Falcons
Massachusetts declares today "New Kids on the Block Day"
Tens of thousands of pro-democracy students strike in Beijing, China
Brian Friel's stage drama "Dancing at Lughnasa" opens at the Abbey Theatre in Dublin, Ireland; later wins Olivier Award, Tony Award, and Drama Desk Award for Best Play
Gruinard Island, Scotland, is officially declared free of anthrax after 48 years of quarantine
STS-31 launches, the 35th mission of the US Space Shuttle program, carrying the Hubble space telescope
West Germany and East Germany agree to merge currencies and economies by July 1
Freddie Stowers is awarded the posthumous Medal of Honor for which he had been recommended in 1918.
Vinson Pike fined £1000 for distributing obscene computer pictures
Firestone World Bowling Tournament of Champions won by George Branham
The IRA explodes a 1000kg car bomb in Bishopsgate, London, killing a news photographer and injuring 44 others
Armando Calderon Sol wins El Salvador presidential election
Bomb attack in center of Johannesburg, 9 killed
NFL Draft: Ohio State defensive tackle Dan Wilkinson first pick by Cincinnati Bengals
NY Rangers sweep NY Islanders in NHL playoffs
Dow Jones Industrial Average hits record 4303.98
Package bomb, linked to Unabomber, blows up killing Gilbert B Murray
Highest scoring MLB baseball game in 17 years - Minnesota Twins 24, Detroit Tigers 11
The UN and Iraq end a third round of negotiations over Iraq's possible sale of $1 billion of oil for 90 days for a 180-day trial period
United States lifts economic sanctions imposed on Libya 18 years ago, as reward for cooperation in eliminating weapons of mass destruction
King Gyanendra of Nepal gives in to the demands of protesters and restores the parliament he dissolved in 2002
Iceland announces that Norway will shoulder the defense of Iceland during peacetime
33 people are killed and 115 are injured after a magnitude 5.7 earthquake strikes Jalalabad, Afghanistan
Deadliest structural failure in history when 1,134, mostly garment workers killed and 2,500 injured after the Rana Plaza building collapses in Savar Upazila, Bangladesh
Armenia commemorates the 100th anniversary of the Armenian Genocide by the Ottoman Empire
Golden State Killer suspect Joseph DeAngelo arrested and charged with eight murders after being identified through genealogy websites
Streaming music services overtake worldwide sales of CDs and vinyl for the first time according to IFPI
Suffragist Millicent Fawcett is the first woman to have a statue erected in Parliament Square, London
At least 82 COVID-19 patients die in a fire at Ibn Khatib hospital in Baghdad, Iraq, with 100 more injured
Slovenian populist prime minister Janez Janša defeated by Freedom Movement 's Robert Golob, a party only launched in January 2022
Violent clashes between Arab nomads and members of the Massalit community in Sudan's West Darfur state result in the deaths of at least 168 people
India surpasses China as the worlds most populous country according to UN estimates, with 1,425,775,850 people (estimated to reach 1.7 billion by 2064)
Gustav Klimt's "Portrait of Fräulein Lieser", previously though lost, sells for a record Austrian auction price of 30 million euros

Amsterdam's Mayor Femke Halsema apologizes for the city’s role in the persecution of its Jewish residents during a Holocaust commemoration at the Hollandsche Schouwburg, a theater that the Nazis used as a deportation center; over 60,000 of the Dutch capital's pre-war Jewish population of 80,000 were deported and killed
Sophie Lloyd, a female magician who tricked her way into British men-only Magic Circle in 1991, is granted membership 34 years after being expelled
Charles III, Duke of Bourbon, on behalf of the Holy Roman Empire, drives the French led by Admiral Bonnivet out of Milan
Battle of Muhlberg: Catholic forces under EmperorCharles Vdefeat Protestant princes John Frederick I and Philip I of Hesse
French revolutionaryJean-Paul Maratis acquitted by the Revolutionary Tribunal of charges brought by the Girondin in Paris
1st performance ofJoseph Haydn's oratorio "Die Jahreszeiten" (The Seasons)
Eastman Kodak founded byGeorge Eastman
French cyclistHenri Desgrangerides 100km in a world record time of 2:39:18
US fleet under commodoreGeorge Deweysails from Hong Kong to Philippines
Transvaal British Uitlanders askQueen Victoriafor aid
President Loubet of France visits KingVictor Emmanuel IIIof Italy and pointedly ignores the Pope, exacerbating relations between France and the Roman Catholic Church
First-class cricket debut of SirJack Hobbs, "The Master" for Surrey v the "Gentlemen of England" (18 & 88)
Ernest Shackletonand five men of the Imperial Trans-Antarctic Expedition launch a lifeboat from uninhabited Elephant Island in the Southern Ocean to organise a rescue for ice-trapped ship Endurance
SudetenGermans in Czechoslovakia demand self government
Miles Davismakes recording debut with the Herbie Fields Orchestra, backing singer "Rubberlegs" Wilson, at Savoy Records Studio, Newark, New Jersey
11 players named to the Baseball Hall of Fame: Tinker, Evers, Chance, Burkett, McCarthy, Waddell, Plank, Walsh,Jack Chesbro, Griffith and McGinnity
One-act ballet "Symphonic Variations" choreographed byFrederick Ashtonwith music by César Franck danced by Sandler's Wells Ballet debuts at Covent Garden, London
Winston Churchillknighted byQueen Elizabeth II
JFKaccepts "sole responsibility" following Bay of Pigs
LA Dodgers pitcherSandy Koufax's 2nd career 18-strikeout, in a 10-2 win over Cubs in Chicago
Future Basketball Hall of Fame point guardBob Cousyplays his last NBA game for Boston as Celtics beat LA Lakers, 112-109 in Game 6 for their 5th straight NBA C'ship
Gene Roddenberryregisters his Star Trek series with the Writers Guild of America

American GeneralWilliam Westmorelandsays in a Vietnam War news conference that the enemy has "gained support in the United States that gives him hope that he can win politically that which he cannot win militarily"
Paul McCartneysays there is no truth to rumors he is dead
AngelsNolan Ryanstrikes out 15 Mariners, 20th time he has 15 in game
US State of Georgia designatesRay Charles' rendition of "Georgia On My Mind" (written byHoagy Carmichaeland Stuart Gorrell) as official state song
Bill Shoemakerwins his 8,000th horse race, 2,000 more than any other jockey
150Khomeinifollowers assault student dormitory in West Germany
Howard Sternholds a free speech rally at Dag Hammarskjold Plaza, NYC
Junk bond kingMichael Milkenpleads guilty to six felonies related to securities fraud
George Steinbrennerdrops his suits against baseball
David Robinsonscores ties 7th highest total in the NBA - 71
Court orders New York Yankees baseball playerDarryl Strawberryto pay back $350,000 in taxes
NFL Draft: Ole Miss quarterbackEli Manningfirst pick by San Diego Chargers
Cardinal Joseph Ratzinger is inaugurated as the 265th Pope of the Roman Catholic Church and takes the name PopeBenedict XVI
Christy Turlington’s directorial debut, "No Woman, No Cry", is released in the U.S.
Ed Sheeran's "÷" is the best-selling album of 2017, selling 6.1 million copies, according to IFPI
US PresidentDonald Trumphosts his first state dinner for visiting French PresidentEmmanuel Macron
Joe Bidenbecomes the first US President to officially recognize killing of Armenians in the Ottoman empire during WWI as 'genocide'
French PresidentEmmanuel Macronwins re-election defeating National Rally'sMarine Le Pen, first sitting French president re-elected in 20 years
Tucker Carlson, Fox News most popular prime-time host, is fired from the cable network

Michelangeloundertakes to carve 12 Apostles for the Cathedral of Florence, each four and a quarter braccia high (248.2cm), at the rate of at least one completed statue per year. He produced only one, of St. Matthew, and that remained unfinished.
Canadian-American adventurer Joshua Slocum sets sail from Boston, Massachusetts on a solo around-the-world voyage aboard 'Spray', an 11.2-m oyster sloop
Spanish–American War: Spain declares war after rejecting US ultimatum to withdraw from Cuba
The Woolworth Building opened in New York City byFrank Winfield Woolworthat a cost of $13.5 million, at 792 feet then the world's tallest building
Easter Rising of Irish republicans against British occupation begins in Dublin
17th century Swedish warship Vasa, which sunk on her maiden voyage in 1628, is salvaged
Gugliemo Gonzaga, Duke of Mantua and Montferrat (1550-87), born in Mantua, Italy (d. 1587)
Vincent de Paul, French Roman Catholic Saint, born in Pouy, Gascony, France (d. 1660)
Benedikt Lechler, German composer, born in Füssen, Duchy of Bavaria (d. 1659)
John Graunt, English statistician, founder of science of demography, born in London (d. 1674)
Jan Peeters (the Elder), Flemish Baroque painter of stormy seascapes, and draughtsman, born in Antwerp, United Provinces of the Netherlands (d. 1677)
Cornelis Dusart, Dutch painter and engraver, born in Haarlem, Netherlands (d. 1704)
Christian Ludwig Boxberg, German composer, born in Sondershausen, Thuringia, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1729)
Giovanni Battista Martini, Italian Conventual Franciscan friar and composer, born in Bologna, Papal States (d. 1784)
Nathaniel Hone, Irish-born portrait and miniature painter, born in Dublin (d. 1784)

Edmund Cartwright, English inventor (power loom), born in Marnham, Nottinghamshire, England (d. 1823)

Robert Bailey Thomas, American journalist and founder (The Farmer's Almanac), born in Grafton, Massachusetts (d. 1846)

Nikolaj Bestuzhev, Russian Navy officer, writer and painter (Decembrist revolt), born in Saint Petersburg (d. 1855)
Karl Leberecht Immermann, German writer, born in Magdeburg (d. 1840)
Georg Hellmesberger Sr, Austrian violinist, conductor (Vienna Philharmonic, 1842-67), and composer, born in Vienna, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1873)
Thomas Oliver Selfridge, American naval officer who served during the Mexican-American War and the Civil War, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1902)
Charles Ferguson Smith, American Major General (Union Army), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1862)
Vicente Fidel López, Argentine historian (La Novia del Hereje), born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 1903)
Anthony Trollope, English novelist and poet (Barchester Towers), born in London (d. 1882)
James E. Harrison, Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Greenville District, South Carolina (d. 1875)
Erastus Barnard Tyler, American businessman, merchant, and Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in West Bloomfield, New York (d. 1891)
François Fournier, Swiss-French postage stamp forger, born in Croix-de-Rozon, Switzerland (d. 1917)
Robert Brank Vance, American politician, U.S. House of Representatives from North Carolina (1873-85), born in Buncombe, North Carolina (d. 1899)
George Peabody Estey, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Hillsborough County, New Hampshire (d. 1881)
Jeltje de Bosch Kemper, Dutch feminist, born in Amsterdam (d. 1916)
Friedrich von Holstein, German civil servant (head of the political department of the German Foreign Office), born in Schwedt, Brandenburg, Germany (d. 1909)
Carl Spitteler, Swiss poet (Prometheus & Epimetheus; Nobel 1919), born in Liestal, Switzerland (d. 1924)
Joseph Gallieni, French general (Battle of Marne) and military governor of Paris, born in Saint-Béat, France (d. 1916)
John Lawson Stoddard, American lecturer, photographer, and author, considered the father of travelogue, born in Brookline, Massachusetts (d. 1931)
Eduardo Acevedo Díaz, Uruguayan writer (Ismael, Grito de Gloria), born in Uruguay, Montevideo (d. 1921)
George August Alexander Alting von Geusau, Dutch Minister of War (1918-20), born in Arnhem, Netherlands (d. 1937)
George Foottit, English clown, born in Manchester, United Kingdom (d. 1921)
Fannie Thomas, American who became the oldest known American at her death (113 years 273 days at death), born in Denver, Illinois (d. 1981)
Blanche Ring, American singer ("In The Good Old Summer Time"), and stage and screen actress, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1961)
Theodor Korner von Siegringen, 5th President of Austria (1951-57), born in Újszőny by Komorn, Austria-Hungary (d. 1957)
John Russell Pope, American architect (Jefferson Memorial, National Archives and Records Administration), born in New York City (d. 1937)
Willem Elisa Roelofs jr., Dutch painter and cartoonist, son of landscape painter Willem Roelofs, born in Belgium (d. 1940)
Jenő Huszka, Hungarian composer (Bob herceg (Prince Bob)), born in Szeged, Hungary (d. 1960)
Erich Raeder, German naval commander (1928–43), born in Wandsbek, Germany (d. 1960)
Charles Cuvillier, French composer of operetta (The Naughty Princess), born in Paris (d. 1955)
Jean Crotti, Swiss-French artist, born in Bulle, Fribourg, Switzerland (d. 1958)
Gideon Sundback, Swedish-American inventor, perfected the zipper, born in Ödestugu Parish, Sweden (d. 1954)[1]
Hugh Dowding, commander of RAF Fighter Command during the Battle of Britain, born in Moffat, Scotland (d. 1970)
Kurt Pinthus, German-American writer, born in Erfurt (d. 1975)
Denys Finch Hatton, English big-game hunter, born in Kensington, London (d. 1931)
Lyubov Popova, Russian avant-garde artist and painter, born in Ivanovskoe, Moscow (d. 1924)
Stafford Cripps, British Labour politician, born in London (d. 1952)
Jack Hulbert, British actor (Into the Blue, Bulldog Jack), born in Ely, Cambridgeshire, England (d. 1978)
Robert Harron, American actor (Birth of a Nation, Intolerance), born in New York City (d. 1920)
Gustav Schmidt, German army general, born in Germany (d. 1943)
Andy Cooper, American Baseball HOF pitcher (East-West All-Star 1933, 36; Negro NL pennant 1929; 3 x Negro AL pennant; Detroit Stars, Kansas City Monarchs), born in Waco, Texas (d. 1941)
Benjamin Lee Whorf, American linguist (the relation of language to thinking and cognition), born in Winthrop, Massachusetts (d. 1941)
György Kósa, Hungarian composer, born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1984)
Manuel Ávila Camacho, 45th President of Mexico (1940-46), born in Teziutlán, Puebla, Mexico (d. 1955)
Oscar Zariski, Russian mathematician (algebraic geometry), born in Kobrin, Russian Empire (d. 1986)
Charles Phillips, British archaeologist who lead the Sutton Hoo excavation, born in England (d. 1985)
Reuben "Rube" Bloom, American pianist, arranger, bandleader (The Bayou Boys), songwriter ("Fools Rush In (Where Angels Fear to Tread)"; "Give Me the Simple Life"), born in New York City (d. 1976)

Siegfried Frederick Nadel, Austrian-British anthropologist (Black Byzantium), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1956)
Willem De Kooning, Dutch artist (North Atlantic Light), born in Rotterdam, Netherlands (d. 1997)
Robert Penn Warren, American poet and novelist (All the King's Men, Pulitzer 1946), born in Guthrie, Kentucky (d. 1989)
Václav Trojan, Czech composer, born in Plzeň, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1983)
Marceline Day [Newlin], American actress, mostly in silent films (The Cameraman; The Jazz Age; The Arm of the Law), born in Colorado Springs (d. 2000)
Bernhard Grzimek, German zoologist (West Germany), born in Neisse, Prussian Silesia, German Empire (d. 1987)
Irven Spence, American animator, born in Lincoln, Nebraska (d. 1995)

Karl Schiller, German economist and politician (Minister of Economics), born in Breslau, Silesia (d. 1994)
Robert Joseph Kane, President of US Olympic Committee (1977-80), born in Ithaca, New York (d. 1992)
Violet Archer [Balestreri], Canadian percussionist, composer, teacher, and organist, born in Montréal, Quebec (d. 2000)
Justin Wilson, American Cajun chef (Wise Potato Chips) and humorist, born in Roseland, Louisiana (d. 2001)
Phil Watson, Canadian NHL right wing (Stanley Cup 1940 [NYR], 1944 [Montreal Canadiens]) and coach (NY Rangers), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1991)
Ruth White, American actress (To Kill a Mockingbird, Up the Down Staircase), born in Perth Amboy, New Jersey (d. 1969)
William Castle, American film director, producer, and actor (Rosemary's Baby, House on Haunted Hill, 13 Ghosts), born in New York (d. 1977)

Jim Hardy, American NFL quarterback (LA Rams; Pro Bowl 1950), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2019)
Clement Freud, British writer, radio personality, and politician, born in Berlin, Germany (d. 2009)
Ruth Kobart [Kahn], American actor and singer (King's Quest VII, Forum), born in Des Moines, Iowa (d. 2002)
Yehoshua Lakner, Israeli pianist, composer (Circles and Signals; Cornerstones), and teacher, born in Bratislava, Slovakia (d. 2003)

Thorbjorn Falldin, Swedish politician, Prime Minister (1976-78, 79-82) and sheep farmer, born in Hogsjo (d. 2016)
"Fatty" George [Pressler], Austrian swing jazz clarinettist, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1982)
Josy Barthel, Luxembourgish 1500m runner (Olympic gold 1952), born in Mamer, Luxembourg (d. 1992)
Pasqualino de Santis, Italian cinematographer, born in Fondi, Latina, Italy (d. 1996)
Seymour Bernstein, American concert pianist, teacher, and composer, born in Newark, New Jersey
Gustav Krivinka, Czech composer, born in Doubravice nad Svitavou, Czech Republic (d. 1990)
Johnny Griffin III, American jazz saxophonist (A Blowin' Session), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2008)

Ferit Tüzün, Turkish composer (Esintiler - Inspirations; The Ears of Midas), and conductor (Turkish State Opera and Ballet Orchestra, 1959-77), born in Istanbul (d. 1977)

Shammi [Nargis Rabadi], Indian actress (Half Ticket, Dekh Bhai Dekh), born in Bombay, British India (d. 2018)

Jerome Callet, American musician and designer of brass instruments, born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 2019)

Richard Donner [Schwartzberg], American television and film director (Twilight Zone; The Omen, Superman; Lethal Weapon; Scrooged), and producer (X-Men; Tales From The Crypt), born in The Bronx, New York (d. 2021)
Rodolfo Micheli, Argentine soccer striker (13 caps; topscorer Copa América 1955; CA Independiente), born in Munro, Argentina (d. 2022)
Bridget Riley, British painter (op-art), born in Norwood London, England
Coen Flink, Dutch actor (Pastorale 1943, Havinck, Honneponnetje), born in Amsterdam (d. 2000)
Vladimir Yengibaryan, Armenian boxer (Olympic gold light welterweight 1956), born in Yerevan, Armenia (d. 2013)
Alan Eagleson, Canadian ice hockey agent and promoter, born in St. Catharines, Canada
Claire Davenport, English actress (The Elephant Man), born in Sale, Cheshire, England (d. 2002)
Freddie Scott, American singer and songwriter ("Cry to Me"), born in Providence, Rhode Island (d. 2007)
Helmuth Lohner, Austrian stage and screen actor and stage director (Mrs. Warren's Profession), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 2015)
Patricia Bosworth, American actor, journalist, writer, and biographer, born in Oakland, California (d. 2020)

Allen Aylett, Australian Rules football midfielder (North Melbourne FC 220 games) and executive (president North Melbourne FC 1971-76, 2001-05; chairman VFL 1977-84), born in Melbourne, Australia (d. 2022)
John Barbour, TV host (Real People), born in Toronto, Ontario
Louis Keith, physician (expert on multiple-births), born in Chicago, Illinois
(Thomas) "Tucker" Smith, American stage and screen dancer, singer and actor (West Side Story - "Ice"), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1988)
Guillermo Escalada, Uruguayan soccer forward (30 caps; Nacional, Gimnasia de La Plata, Montevideo Wanderers FC), born in Montevideo, Uruguay (d. 2023)

David Larter, Scottish cricket fast bowler (10 Tests England, 37 wickets; Northamptonshire), born in Inverness, Scotland
Michael Parks, American actor (Tusk, Then Came Bronson), born in Corona California (d. 2017)
Sue Grafton, American author of detective novels ("A" Is for Alibi), born in Louisville, Kentucky (d. 2017)
John Williams, Australian classical guitarist, born in Melbourne, Victoria
Richard Holbrooke, American diplomat (Assistant Secretary of State, UN Ambassador) and author, born in New York City (d. 2010)
George Vella, Maltese politician, President of Malta (2019-), born in Żejtun, Malta
Valeri Abramovich Voloshin, Russian pilot cosmonaut, born in Yangiyul, Tashkent
Richard Sterban, American country singer (Oak Ridge Boys - "Elvira"), born in Camden, New Jersey
(Bernard) St. Clair Lee, American pop singer (The Hues Corporation - "Rock The Boat"), born in San Francisco, California (d. 2011)
John McDonald, Australian rugby league centre (13 Tests; Queensland; Manly-Warringah RLFC), born in Toowoomba, Australia (d. 2023)
Tony Visconti, American music producer (David Bowie - Blackstar; Low; "Heroes"; Osibisa), and bassist, born in Brooklyn, New York City
